source_document: Executive Order 14110 (Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence)
section_path: Section 4.2 - Ensuring Safe and Reliable AI
passage_text: Within 90 days of the date of this order, and on an ongoing basis thereafter, any company developing or demonstrating an intent to develop a potential dual-use foundation model shall report to the Federal Government, on an ongoing basis: the activities related to training of the model, including the physical and cybersecurity protections taken to assure the integrity of that training process against sophisticated threats; the ownership and possession of the model weights of any dual-use foundation models, and the physical and cybersecurity measures taken to protect those model weights; and the results of any developed red-team testing related to lowering barriers to entry for the development of biological or nuclear weapons, the discovery of software vulnerabilities, or other threats to national security.

obligation.subject: any company developing or demonstrating an intent to develop a potential dual-use foundation model
obligation.action: report to the Federal Government activities related to model training including cybersecurity protections, ownership and possession of model weights, and results of red-team testing related to biological/nuclear weapons, software vulnerabilities, or national security threats

timeline.timeline_text: Within 90 days of the date of this order, and on an ongoing basis thereafter

affected_obligations: Section 4.2 - safety reporting to federal government
ambiguity.interpretation_notes: The qualifier 'potential' before 'dual-use foundation model' creates uncertainty about at what stage of development the reporting obligation attaches. Combined with 'demonstrating an intent to develop,' this could capture companies very early in the R&D process before any model exists.
ambiguity.suggested_clarification: Define objective criteria for when a model under development becomes a 'potential' dual-use foundation model triggering the reporting requirement, such as compute thresholds or capability benchmarks
